title: Coronation chicken
subtitle: Cold poached chicken in curried mayonnaise
excerpt: Cold poached chicken pieces dressed in a curried mayonnaise sauce flavoured with apricot jam, mango chutney, and mild curry powder. Created for Queen Elizabeth II's coronation banquet in 1953, it became one of the most widely reproduced dishes in British cooking, a cold buffet staple that distils the Anglo-Indian flavour combination of sweet fruit and warm spice into a versatile sauce.
sourceNotes: Coronation chicken was created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume of the Cordon Bleu cookery school for Queen Elizabeth II's coronation banquet, June 1953. The recipe was published in 'The Constance Spry Cookery Book' (1956). It became a British buffet standard and has been adapted in dozens of versions.

content.text: The sauce for coronation chicken should be assertive, the curry powder and fruit elements both need to be present. A timid version, where the spice is barely detectable and the fruit is minimal, produces a disappointing beige mayonnaise. Taste the sauce before adding the chicken: it should smell of curry, taste sweet-and-warm, and have a clear apricot note.

Poach the chicken gently, simmering in barely-trembling water for 20 minutes produces moist, tender meat that pulls apart into large pieces. Boiling produces tight, stringy chicken. Cool completely before dressing: warm chicken will make the mayonnaise sauce oily and thin.
Poach the chicken and make the sauce

Place the {{chicken-breasts}} in a pan with the {{onion}}, {{bay-leaf}}, and enough cold water to cover. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Poach for 18 to 20 minutes until cooked through, the internal temperature should reach 74°C or the juices run clear when pierced. Remove and leave to cool completely in the poaching liquid, then pull into large pieces or slice. Meanwhile, cook the {{curry-powder}} in a dry pan over low heat for 1 minute until fragrant. Cool. Combine the {{mayonnaise}}, {{plain-yoghurt}}, cooked curry powder, {{mango-chutney}}, {{apricot-jam}}, and lemon juice. Season with salt and taste: it should be noticeably spiced, sweet, and tangy.
Add the cooled chicken to the sauce and fold gently to coat. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes. Serve on a bed of watercress or rocket, or as a sandwich filling in a soft white roll.

items.fix: Cool the chicken completely (1 hour at room temperature or 30 minutes in the fridge) before adding to the sauce. Warm chicken liquefies mayonnaise
Toasting the curry powder in a dry pan briefly develops its flavour. The mango chutney and apricot jam must both be detectable, add another tablespoon of chutney if the sauce lacks sweetness
items.cause: Curry powder not toasted or insufficient chutney

items.fix: Poach at a bare simmer, the water should barely shiver. Pull at 74°C internal; overcooked chicken tightens and becomes fibrous
items.cause: Boiled rather than gently poached, or overcooked

items.fix: Add a splash more lemon juice to balance. The sweet-savoury ratio should be close, neither should dominate

content.text: Coronation chicken has a clear starting point. It was created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume for the 1953 coronation banquet. Within ten years it was on every British buffet table: weddings, work lunches, garden parties. The reason it caught on is simple. The mild curry spice and apricot sweetness sit comfortably with familiar Western ingredients. It bridges two traditions and works every time.
